content.body: Mixtures can be separated using physical methods that exploit differences in properties such as particle size, solubility, boiling point, or density.
FILTRATION: Separates an insoluble solid from a liquid. The solid is trapped in filter paper; the liquid (filtrate) passes through. Example: separating sand from water.
EVAPORATION: Removes water (solvent) from a solution by heating, leaving the dissolved solid (solute) behind. Example: getting salt from seawater.

CRYSTALLISATION: A solution is heated to concentrate it then cooled slowly to form pure crystals. Better than evaporation to dryness as it gives purer product. Example: purifying copper sulphate or sugar.

content.title: Procedure for Crystallisation
content.body:
1. Dissolve solute in hot water to make saturated solution
2. Filter to remove impurities
3. Evaporate gently until crystals start to form
4. Cool the solution slowly
5. Filter off crystals and dry between filter paper

questions.question: Filtration separates:
questions.explanation: Filtration traps insoluble (undissolved) solids in filter paper while liquid passes through.

questions.question: The liquid that passes through filter paper is called:
questions.explanation: The liquid collected after filtration is the filtrate. The solid left behind is the residue.

questions.question: To separate salt from seawater, the best method is:
questions.explanation: Salt is dissolved in seawater (soluble). Evaporation drives off the water, leaving salt crystals.

questions.question: Crystallisation is preferred over evaporation to dryness because:
questions.explanation: Slow crystallisation produces regular, pure crystals. Rapid evaporation traps impurities.

questions.question: In filtration, insoluble solid trapped in filter paper is called:
questions.explanation: The solid that cannot pass through the filter paper is called the residue.

questions.question: Which property difference does filtration exploit?
questions.explanation: Filter paper has tiny pores. Solid particles too large pass through; liquid molecules are small enough.

questions.question: Which method gets pure water from a salt solution?
questions.explanation: Distillation collects the evaporated water by condensation, giving pure water. Evaporation leaves the water behind.

questions.question: Cooling a hot saturated solution slowly causes:
questions.explanation: As temperature drops, less solute can stay dissolved — excess solute crystallises out.

questions.question: Evaporation separates:
questions.explanation: Evaporation heats the solution, driving off the solvent and leaving the dissolved solid.

questions.question: Sand and water mixture is separated by:
questions.explanation: Sand does not dissolve in water (insoluble). Filtration separates insoluble solids from liquids.

questions.question: In crystallisation, after filtering off crystals, they are:
questions.explanation: Crystals are patted dry between filter paper to remove surface moisture without dissolving them.

questions.question: A saturated solution is one that:
options: Cannot dissolve any more solute at that temperature
questions.answer: Cannot dissolve any more solute at that temperature
questions.explanation: At saturation point, the solution holds the maximum amount of dissolved solute.

questions.question: Evaporating seawater to get table salt — the salt is the:
questions.explanation: Salt is dissolved in water. Water = solvent. Salt = solute. After evaporation, salt remains.

questions.question: Which technique would you use to separate a mixture of chalk and water?
questions.explanation: Chalk is insoluble in water — filtration traps chalk particles in filter paper.

questions.question: During crystallisation, cooling should be:
questions.explanation: Slow cooling allows large, pure, well-formed crystals to grow. Fast cooling gives small, impure crystals.

questions.question: Copper(II) sulphate crystals are blue. If solution is evaporated too fast:
questions.explanation: Rapid evaporation traps impurities inside forming crystals, reducing purity.

questions.question: To remove suspended clay particles from muddy water, use:
questions.explanation: Clay is insoluble and forms a suspension. Filter paper traps clay particles.

questions.question: After evaporating salt solution to near dryness, you see crystals forming. This is because:
questions.explanation: As water evaporates, same amount of salt in less water → more concentrated → crystals form.

questions.question: Which does NOT belong: filtration, crystallisation, evaporation, magnetism?
questions.explanation: Magnetism separates magnetic materials from non-magnetic. The others involve liquid-solid separations.

questions.question: Why is filter paper folded into a cone shape?
options: To create a better seal in the funnel and increase filtration area
questions.answer: To create a better seal in the funnel and increase filtration area
questions.explanation: The cone shape fits the funnel, creating a seal. Folding also increases surface area for faster filtration.

content.body: DISTILLATION: Separates liquids with different boiling points, or pure liquid from dissolved solids. The liquid is boiled; vapour is condensed and collected as distillate.

FRACTIONAL DISTILLATION: Used when two miscible liquids have close but different boiling points. A fractionating column is used. Example: separating ethanol(78°C) from water(100°C), refining crude oil.
PAPER CHROMATOGRAPHY: Separates soluble mixtures based on how strongly components are attracted to the paper vs solvent. Each substance travels a different distance.

Rf value = Distance travelled by substance ÷ Distance travelled by solvent front
Rf is always between 0 and 1

content.title: Chromatography Rf
content.body:
Solvent travels 10cm. Ink spot travels 7cm.
Rf = 7÷10 = 0.7
This Rf value identifies the substance.

questions.question: Distillation separates liquids based on differences in:
questions.explanation: Distillation works because different liquids boil (and vaporise) at different temperatures.

questions.question: The liquid collected after condensation in distillation is called the:
questions.explanation: The condensed vapour that is collected is the distillate.

questions.question: Fractional distillation differs from simple distillation in using:
questions.explanation: The fractionating column separates mixtures with close boiling points by allowing repeated condensation and re-evaporation.

questions.question: Crude oil is separated into fractions using:
questions.explanation: Crude oil contains many hydrocarbons with different boiling points — fractional distillation separates them.

questions.question: In paper chromatography, the stationary phase is:
questions.explanation: Stationary phase = what stays still (paper). Mobile phase = what moves (solvent).

questions.question: Rf value formula is:
questions.explanation: Rf = distance moved by substance ÷ distance moved by solvent front

questions.question: If Rf = 1, the substance:
questions.explanation: Rf = 1 means substance moved same distance as solvent — very soluble in mobile phase, not attracted to paper.

questions.question: Rf = 0 means:
questions.explanation: Rf = 0 means no movement — very strongly attracted to paper, not to solvent.

questions.question: To get pure water from copper sulphate solution, use:
questions.explanation: Water (bp 100°C) can be evaporated and condensed to collect pure water. Copper sulphate stays behind.

questions.question: Chromatography is used to:
options: Separate soluble mixtures by how far components travel
questions.answer: Separate soluble mixtures by how far components travel
questions.explanation: Each component has a characteristic Rf value — chromatography separates and identifies them.

questions.question: Substance travels 6cm, solvent front travels 8cm. Rf =
Why must the ink/spot in chromatography be above the solvent level?
options: So it doesn't dissolve directly into the solvent tank
questions.answer: So it doesn't dissolve directly into the solvent tank
questions.explanation: If spot is in the solvent, it washes away instantly instead of being carried up the paper gradually.

questions.question: Water and alcohol (ethanol) mixture: alcohol boils at 78°C, water at 100°C. Which distils over first?
questions.explanation: Lower boiling point = distils first. Alcohol (78°C) comes over before water (100°C).

questions.question: A condenser in distillation apparatus:
questions.explanation: Cold water flows around the condenser, cooling vapour and condensing it back to liquid.

questions.question: Chromatography can tell if a substance is pure because:
options: Pure substances show only one spot
questions.answer: Pure substances show only one spot
questions.explanation: A pure substance produces a single spot. Multiple spots indicate a mixture of components.

questions.question: The mobile phase in paper chromatography is:
options: The solvent moving up the paper
questions.answer: The solvent moving up the paper
questions.explanation: Mobile phase = the solvent that moves (water, ethanol, etc). It carries solutes with it.

questions.question: Simple distillation is best for:
options: Two liquids with close boiling points
Separating a dissolved solid from liquid/collecting pure solvent
questions.answer: Separating a dissolved solid from liquid/collecting pure solvent
questions.explanation: Simple distillation works when the difference in boiling point is large (e.g. salt solution → pure water).

questions.question: In what order do crude oil fractions leave a fractionating column?
questions.explanation: Fractions with lowest boiling points (petrol, LPG) come off at top of column first. Heavy oils come at bottom.

questions.question: Rf values are useful because:
options: Different substances have characteristic Rf values for identification
They are the same in all solvents
questions.answer: Different substances have characteristic Rf values for identification
questions.explanation: Each substance has a specific Rf in a given solvent — like a fingerprint used to identify it.

questions.question: Which separation technique uses capillary action to move solvent up paper?
questions.explanation: Capillary action draws solvent up the paper in paper chromatography, carrying dissolved substances with it.
